title: Credential Compromise: Card Deployed Across Atypical Merchant Category Mix
narrative: Fraudster uses stolen card credentials at merchants that dramatically differ from the cardholder's established spending habits. While individual transaction amounts and timing appear normal, the card is suddenly being used across an unusually diverse set of merchant category codes and product types. C13 and C14 counters reveal the card is transacting at far more merchant categories than the legitimate owner's historical pattern. The card may have only been used at 2-3 MCC categories historically, but now appears at 15+ categories in the rolling window. Device fingerprint is new to this card (D1=0), but behavioral signals like transaction amount and frequency appear individually unremarkable. This pattern exploits credential theft where the fraudster conducts reconnaissance to avoid detection thresholds, spacing transactions reasonably but betraying themselves through merchant-category diversity incompatible with the real cardholder's lifestyle.
indicators: D1 = 0 indicating first-time pairing of this device with the card
C13 > 24 (p90), card used across many distinct merchant category codes in rolling window
C14 > 7 (p90), card transacting at elevated number of unique merchant IDs
C11 > 27 (p90), card activity counter far exceeds typical cardholder baseline
card1_distinct_addr1 between 3 and 8, multiple shipping addresses in recent window
TransactionAmt between 60 and 180, amounts within normal per-transaction range
C8 / C13 ratio < 0.4, indicating merchant category diversity far exceeds address diversity
indicator_explanations: The device has never been seen with this card before
Card is being used across many more merchant types than cardholder's normal pattern
Card appears at an unusually high number of different merchants in the time window
Card-level activity counter is far above the legitimate cardholder's established baseline
Multiple different shipping addresses used recently, inconsistent with single-household purchasing
Individual transaction amounts fit normal spending range, masking the fraud surface
Merchant category spread is disproportionately wide compared to address diversity, suggesting catalog testing
summary: Stolen card credentials are being used across a merchant category mix that is completely inconsistent with the legitimate cardholder's established spending pattern. While individual transactions appear normal in amount and timing, aggregated counters reveal the card is suddenly transacting across far more merchant types and locations than the real owner's history supports.
reasoning: Legitimate cardholders exhibit stable merchant-category preferences over time. When a card suddenly appears at 20+ different MCC categories with a new device while amounts remain unremarkable, it indicates a fraudster conducting low-and-slow testing across multiple fraud-friendly merchant types. The disproportionate merchant diversity relative to address diversity suggests systematic credential exploitation rather than organic spending pattern evolution.
